votes.notes: The learner discovers they used a public banded version of the data instead of the restricted continuous version, but never identifies that they downloaded a namesake dataset sharing an acronym from a separate repository as specified in the hidden truth.

The learner discovered they used a public version instead of a restricted version of the same UKDS dataset, rather than discovering that they downloaded an entirely different dataset sharing the same acronym.

votes.learner_evidence: That's both. The restricted continuous variable for their continuous measure, and the log transformation for their regression — I matched neither. The eleven percent is the distance between my construction choices and theirs. That's not their number being wrong.

The learner realizes they used a different (public vs restricted UKDS) dataset and mismatched variable construction, but never arrives at the specific mechanism that two distinct datasets share the same short acronym and they downloaded the namesake from a different repository.

votes.learner_evidence: One name search. That's it. I didn't verify the accession, I didn't check the tier — I searched the short name and took whatever the portal surfaced.

votes.notes: The learner successfully grasps the hidden truth, but only after the tutor explicitly reveals the namesake dataset mismatch and separate repositories to them.
